title: Duplicate IP or MAC on OT Switch ‚Äî Segmentation Violation

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CR 5.1.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that IEC 62443 CR 5.1 is enforced ‚Äî Splunk UC-22.15.41: Duplicate IP or MAC on OT Switch ‚Äî Segmentation Violation.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.15.41' running on cisco:ios ARP, claroty:alert, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted ‚Äî SME review required.

spl:
index=net sourcetype="cisco:ios" "%Duplicate address%" OR "%ARP collision%" earliest=-7d
| append [ search index=ot_sec sourcetype="claroty:alert" signature="*ARP*" earliest=-7d ]
| stats count by host, vlan, signature, src_ip
| sort - count
description: Surfaces ARP/ND anomalies that indicate bridging or misconfiguration undermining component-level network segmentation.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for IEC 62443 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes net, ot_sec and sourcetypes cisco:ios, claroty:alert. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Enable DHCP snooping; (2) BPDU guard on access ports; (3) correlate with change tickets; (4) physical inspection for rogue bridges. Indexes required: `index=net`, `index=ot_sec`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=cisco:ios`, `sourcetype=claroty:alert`.
visualization: Table, Topology panel (if ITSI), Time chart. Key fields: `host`, `signature`, `src_ip`, `vlan`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.src | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern ‚Äî correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Switch syslog, Claroty.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `cisco:ios` ARP, `claroty:alert`.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
(1) Enable DHCP snooping; (2) BPDU guard on access ports; (3) correlate with change tickets; (4) physical inspection for rogue bridges.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=net sourcetype="cisco:ios" "%Duplicate address%" OR "%ARP collision%" earliest=-7d
| append [ search index=ot_sec sourcetype="claroty:alert" signature="*ARP*" earliest=-7d ]
| stats count by host, vlan, signature, src_ip
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Duplicate IP or MAC on OT Switch ‚Äî Segmentation Violation (IEC 62443-4-2 / CR 5.1)** ‚Äî Surfaces ARP/ND anomalies that indicate bridging or misconfiguration undermining component-level network segmentation.

Documented **Data sources**: `cisco:ios` ARP, `claroty:alert`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Switch syslog, Claroty. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: net; **sourcetype**: cisco:ios. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=net, sourcetype="cisco:ios", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ Appends rows from a subsearch with `append`.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, vlan, signature, src_ip** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.src | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Duplicate IP or MAC on OT Switch ‚Äî Segmentation Violation (IEC 62443-4-2 / CR 5.1)** ‚Äî Surfaces ARP/ND anomalies that indicate bridging or misconfiguration undermining component-level network segmentation.

Documented **Data sources**: `cisco:ios` ARP, `claroty:alert`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Switch syslog, Claroty. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` ‚Äî enable acceleration for that model.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table, Topology panel (if ITSI), Time chart.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for surfaces arp/nd anomalies that indicate bridging or misconfiguration undermining component-level network segmentation. This matters because it catches problems that could lead to audit findings or compliance gaps.
